name: Major Trauma / MVC
summary: High-energy blunt mechanism with potential for occult life-threatening hemorrhage and multisystem injury.

triage: Apply primary survey ABCDE on arrival; do not delay for registration (EMTALA)
Obtain mechanism: speed, restraint, airbag, ejection, rollover, death at scene
Full set of vitals + SpO2; recheck q5min if unstable
Activate trauma protocol/transfer pathway early given FSED resource limits
nursing: Expose patient fully; maintain normothermia with warming blanket
Maintain spinal motion restriction until cleared
Two large-bore (16-18G) IVs; draw labs on access
Strict I/O; place patient on continuous monitoring
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, automated BP q5min
High-flow O2 to keep SpO2 >=94%
Two large-bore IVs; initiate warmed isotonic fluids judiciously
12-lead ECG if blunt cardiac injury suspected
NPO; foley if no urethral injury*; if indicated
labs: VBG, ethanol, urine/serum tox, hCG in women of childbearing age
imaging: FAST ultrasound for free fluid/pericardial blood
Portable CXR and pelvic X-ray
CT pan-scan (head/c-spine/chest/abd/pelvis)* if indicated and available
Transfer for CT if not on-site
meds: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV over 10 min then 1 g over 8 h if hemorrhagic and <3 h from injury
Fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV q5-10min titrated for pain
Ondansetron 4 mg IV for nausea
Tdap/Td if wounds present and not up to date
reassess: Repeat primary survey after each intervention
Trend lactate, base deficit, hemodynamics for occult shock
Reassess GCS and pupils serially
Repeat FAST if deterioration
disposition: Emergent transfer to Level I/II trauma center for unstable or operative injury
Initiate massive transfusion/blood product if available while arranging transfer
Air vs ground transport based on stability and distance
discharge: Discharge only if truly minor mechanism with negative workup and reliable observation
Return precautions: worsening pain, dyspnea, abdominal distension, syncope
Provide written instructions and 24-h contact
followup: Trauma clinic or PCP within 24-48 h for cleared patients
Repeat imaging if delayed injury suspected
Document advice to avoid driving while on opioids

redFlags: SBP <90, tachycardia, or rising lactate suggesting occult hemorrhage
Distended/rigid abdomen or positive FAST
GCS <=8 or rapidly declining mental status
Mechanism: ejection, rollover, death of co-occupant, prolonged extrication
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization required before any transfer
FSED is non-operative: emergent appropriate-transfer with physician certification of benefit > risk
Send copies of records, imaging, and labs with patient; physician-to-physician acceptance documented
Report MVC injuries per state requirements; preserve evidence if suspected impairment/crime

name: Head & Neck Trauma
summary: Blunt or penetrating injury to head/neck with risk of intracranial bleed, c-spine injury, or airway compromise.

triage: Assess GCS and pupils immediately; recheck serially
Maintain c-spine precautions until clinically/radiographically cleared
Identify anticoagulant/antiplatelet use and bleeding disorders
Screen for LOC, amnesia, vomiting, seizure, severe headache
nursing: Elevate head of bed 30 degrees if no spinal contraindication
Neuro checks q15-30min and document trend
IV access; keep NPO pending imaging
Control external scalp bleeding with direct pressure
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor and continuous SpO2
12-lead ECG if syncope/cardiac cause of fall
IV access; O2 to keep SpO2 >=94%
Cervical collar; spinal motion restriction
NPO until cleared
labs: CBC, CMP, coagulation panel (esp. if on anticoagulants)
Glucose to exclude metabolic cause of AMS
imaging: Non-contrast head CT per Canadian CT Head Rule
CT c-spine per NEXUS/Canadian C-spine criteria
CTA neck* if penetrating or seatbelt sign; if indicated
Transfer for CT if unavailable on-site
meds: Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO/IV for headache
Ondansetron 4 mg IV for vomiting
Reverse anticoagulation if ICH (e.g., 4-factor PCC 25-50 units/kg, vitamin K 10 mg IV)* if indicated
Keppra/levetiracetam 1000 mg IV for seizure prophylaxis in significant TBI* if indicated
reassess: Serial GCS, pupils, and focal neuro exam
Watch for Cushing response (HTN, bradycardia, irregular respirations)
Reassess for delayed bleed in anticoagulated patients
Repeat CT if neuro decline
disposition: Emergent transfer to facility with neurosurgery for any ICH or unstable c-spine
Intubate for GCS <=8 prior to transfer
Admit/observe minor TBI per shared decision if observation capacity exists
discharge: Discharge if negative CT, GCS 15, normal exam, reliable observer
Concussion precautions and graded return-to-activity instructions
Strict return precautions: worsening headache, repeated vomiting, confusion, weakness
followup: PCP or concussion clinic within 1 week
Neurosurgery follow-up if any abnormal finding
Avoid driving/anticoagulant resumption until cleared* if indicated

redFlags: GCS <=8, anisocoria, or new focal deficit
Anticoagulated patient with any intracranial bleed
Suspected expanding hematoma or basilar skull fracture signs
Penetrating neck wound with expanding hematoma, bruit, or airway distress
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization; appropriate-transfer with neurosurgery acceptance documented
FSED lacks neurosurgery/ICU: low threshold for transfer of any ICH
Send CT images and reversal-agent administration record with patient
Report assault-related injuries and suspected abuse per Texas mandatory reporting

name: Penetrating Trauma
summary: Stab/GSW/impalement with risk of vascular, visceral, or cavity injury and rapid exsanguination.

triage: Primary survey; control compressible hemorrhage immediately with direct pressure/tourniquet
Identify wound number, location, weapon/caliber, and time of injury
Do not remove impaled objects; stabilize in place
Activate transfer/trauma pathway immediately for torso/junctional wounds
nursing: Expose fully; log-roll to find exit/posterior wounds; count all wounds
Two large-bore IVs; draw labs and type & cross
Apply occlusive dressing to open chest wounds
Continuous monitoring and frequent vitals
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, BP q5min
High-flow O2 to keep SpO2 >=94%
Two large-bore IVs; warmed fluids/blood
12-lead ECG for precordial wounds (cardiac box)
NPO; tourniquet for extremity hemorrhage* if indicated
imaging: FAST for pericardial/peritoneal blood
CXR for pneumo/hemothorax (mark wounds with radiopaque markers)
CT angiography* if hemodynamically stable and available; if indicated
Transfer for definitive imaging/OR if unstable
meds: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV over 10 min if hemorrhagic and <3 h
Fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV titrated for pain
Cefazolin 2 g IV for wound prophylaxis* if indicated
Tdap/Td if not up to date
reassess: Repeat primary survey and hemodynamics frequently
Reassess for tension pneumothorax (decompress if present)
Trend lactate and mental status
Repeat FAST if deterioration
disposition: Emergent transfer to trauma center/OR for torso, vascular, or cardiac box wounds
Needle/finger decompression and chest tube if tension pneumothorax
Initiate blood products while arranging transport
discharge: Discharge only isolated superficial extremity wounds with negative workup
Wound care, infection precautions, and return instructions
Tetanus status documented
followup: Wound recheck in 48 h
Surgery/trauma follow-up for any deep or cavity-adjacent wound
Suture/staple removal timeline given

redFlags: Hemodynamic instability or positive FAST
Wound to cardiac box, neck zones, or junctional region
Expanding hematoma, pulsatile bleeding, or absent distal pulses
Evisceration or peritonitis
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization before transfer; certify benefit > risk
FSED is non-operative: emergent appropriate-transfer to trauma center is standard
Mandatory law-enforcement reporting of gunshot and stab wounds in Texas; preserve clothing/evidence
Document chain of custody and notify police; send all records with patient

name: Fall
summary: Fall with injury spectrum from minor contusion to occult fracture/ICH, especially in elderly or anticoagulated.

triage: Determine height, surface, mechanism, and whether syncope/medical cause preceded
Identify anticoagulant/antiplatelet use and osteoporosis risk
Assess GCS, focal pain, and ability to bear weight
Screen elderly for elder abuse and recurrent falls
nursing: Vitals including orthostatics if syncope suspected
C-spine precautions if axial pain or AMS
IV access if significant injury anticipated
Pain assessment and immobilize suspected fractures
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor if syncope/medical cause suspected
12-lead ECG to evaluate for arrhythmia/cardiac syncope
IV access* if indicated
Point-of-care glucose
NPO if surgical injury likely* if indicated
labs: Troponin/BNP if cardiac syncope suspected* if indicated
UA and infection workup if precipitating illness suspected* if indicated
imaging: Targeted X-rays of injured extremity/pelvis/hip
Non-contrast head CT if anticoagulated, AMS, or head strike (per CT Head Rule)
CT c-spine per NEXUS/Canadian criteria* if indicated
Transfer for CT if unavailable
meds: Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO/IV for pain
Fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV or morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV for fracture pain
Reverse anticoagulation if ICH (4-factor PCC)* if indicated
Tdap/Td if open wound and not up to date
reassess: Serial neuro checks if head strike/anticoagulated
Reassess pain control and neurovascular status of injured limb
Re-evaluate for delayed bleed in elderly on blood thinners
Reassess gait/safety before discharge
disposition: Transfer for ortho/neurosurgery for hip fracture, unstable fracture, or ICH
Admit/observe for high fall risk or precipitating medical cause
Arrange safe discharge with assistive devices and home assessment
discharge: Discharge if negative workup, ambulatory, and safe home environment
Fall-prevention counseling and review of medications
Return precautions for worsening pain, new neuro symptoms, or repeat fall
followup: PCP within 1 week for fall risk and medication review
Orthopedics for fractures; physical therapy referral
Consider DEXA/osteoporosis evaluation in elderly

redFlags: Anticoagulated patient with head strike (delayed ICH risk)
Inability to bear weight, deformity, or neurovascular compromise
Syncope/medical cause requiring cardiac workup
Signs of elder abuse or inconsistent history
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization; appropriate-transfer for operative fractures/ICH
FSED lacks ortho/neurosurgery: arrange accepting facility and send imaging
Mandatory reporting of suspected elder abuse/neglect to Texas APS
Document home-safety and fall-prevention counseling

name: Laceration
summary: Traumatic skin/soft-tissue wound requiring hemostasis, exploration, and closure with infection prevention.

triage: Control bleeding with direct pressure; elevate
Assess mechanism, contamination, time since injury, and bite/crush component
Screen for tendon/nerve/vessel involvement and retained foreign body
Document tetanus immunization status
nursing: Irrigate and clean wound; assess depth
Neurovascular and motor check distal to wound
Pain control prior to exploration
Prepare repair tray and appropriate suture
keyOrders: Wound irrigation with normal saline (tap water acceptable)
Hemostasis with direct pressure/lidocaine with epi (avoid in end-arteries)
Neurovascular and tendon function exam
Tetanus prophylaxis order
X-ray for radiopaque foreign body* if indicated
labs: CBC if significant blood loss* if indicated
Coagulation panel if anticoagulated and bleeding* if indicated
Wound culture only if clearly infected* if indicated
imaging: Plain X-ray if glass/metal foreign body suspected
Ultrasound for radiolucent (wood/organic) foreign body* if indicated
No imaging for clean simple lacerations
CT for deep/complex facial or suspected open fracture* if indicated
meds: Lidocaine 1% (with epi except digits/tip of nose/ears/penis) for local anesthesia
Tdap/Td if >5 y (dirty) or >10 y (clean); TIG if high-risk and unimmunized
Cephalexin 500 mg PO QID for high-risk/contaminated wounds* if indicated
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO for pain
reassess: Confirm hemostasis and approximation after closure
Recheck distal neurovascular status post-repair
Assess for adequate anesthesia before suturing
Reassess wound tension and need for layered closure
disposition: Discharge most after repair
Refer/transfer for specialist repair (deep facial, tendon, nerve, vascular)
Consider hand surgery for complex hand/flexor tendon injuries
discharge: Wound care: keep clean/dry 24-48 h, watch for infection
Suture removal schedule (face 5 d, scalp/trunk 7-10 d, extremity 10-14 d)
Return precautions: redness, pus, spreading erythema, fever
followup: PCP or wound clinic for suture/staple removal
Hand/plastic surgery for complex repairs
Recheck in 48 h if high infection risk

redFlags: Arterial bleeding or expanding hematoma
Tendon, nerve, or joint capsule involvement
Signs of deep-space infection or necrotizing process
Heavily contaminated/bite wound over hand or near joint
compliance: EMTALA MSE for all presenting patients regardless of payment
FSED appropriate-transfer if specialist repair beyond capability
Report assault- or abuse-related wounds per Texas mandatory reporting
Document tetanus status and wound-care instructions given

name: Burns
summary: Thermal/chemical/electrical injury with risk of airway compromise, fluid loss, and need for burn-center transfer.

triage: Stop the burning process; remove clothing/jewelry; brush off dry chemicals
Assess airway for inhalation injury (soot, singed hairs, hoarseness, stridor)
Estimate %TBSA (Rule of Nines) and depth; note circumferential burns
Identify mechanism: enclosed-space fire, electrical, chemical
nursing: Cover burns with clean dry sheets; maintain normothermia
Large-bore IV access through unburned skin if possible
Continuous monitoring; foley for large-TBSA fluid tracking
Irrigate chemical burns copiously; remove contact lenses for eye exposure
keyOrders: High-flow O2; consider early airway control if inhalation injury
Cardiac monitor (esp. electrical burns for arrhythmia)
Two large-bore IVs; begin lactated Ringer's per Parkland
12-lead ECG for electrical injury
NPO; foley for TBSA >20%* if indicated
labs: Carboxyhemoglobin and ABG/VBG if enclosed-space fire
CK and UA for myoglobinuria (electrical/deep burns)
Coagulation panel and type & screen if extensive* if indicated
imaging: CXR if inhalation injury suspected
ECG/cardiac monitoring for electrical injury
Imaging for associated trauma (e.g., blast/fall)* if indicated
No routine imaging for isolated minor burns
meds: Lactated Ringer's: Parkland 2-4 mL/kg/%TBSA, half over first 8 h from time of burn
Fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV titrated for pain
100% oxygen for suspected CO poisoning; cyanide kit (hydroxocobalamin 5 g IV)* if indicated
Tdap/Td if not up to date; topical bacitracin/silver sulfadiazine for minor burns
reassess: Reassess airway repeatedly; intubate early if progressive edema
Monitor urine output goal 0.5 mL/kg/h (adult) and titrate fluids
Recheck distal perfusion for circumferential burns (escharotomy need)
Trend lactate and acid-base status
disposition: Transfer to burn center per ABA criteria (>10% TBSA, full-thickness, face/hands/feet/perineum/joints, electrical/chemical, inhalation)
Begin resuscitation and pain control before transport
Admit/observe minor burns only if reliable outpatient care unavailable
discharge: Discharge minor superficial/partial-thickness burns with wound care
Daily dressing changes; signs of infection to watch for
Adequate oral analgesia and hydration instructions
followup: Burn clinic or PCP within 24-48 h for wound recheck
Earlier follow-up for hand/face burns
Reassess depth at 48-72 h as burns evolve

redFlags: Stridor, hoarseness, soot in airway, or facial/neck burns (impending airway loss)
Circumferential full-thickness burns with distal ischemia
TBSA >10-20%, electrical, or chemical burns
Elevated carboxyhemoglobin or suspected cyanide toxicity
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization; secure airway before transfer if at risk
FSED appropriate-transfer to ABA-verified burn center with documented acceptance
Report suspected abuse (esp. inconsistent pattern, immersion scald) per Texas law
Send fluid-resuscitation record, TBSA estimate, and time of burn with patient

name: Traumatic Amputation
summary: Partial/complete loss of digit or limb with hemorrhage risk and time-sensitive replantation potential.

triage: Control hemorrhage with direct pressure then tourniquet if uncontrolled
Note time of injury and warm/cold ischemia clock for amputated part
Identify mechanism (sharp/avulsion/crush) affecting replant candidacy
Activate hand/replant center pathway immediately
nursing: Wrap amputated part in saline-moistened gauze, seal in bag, place on ice (do NOT freeze or submerge directly)
Two large-bore IVs; draw labs and type & screen
Elevate and immobilize stump; document neurovascular status
Continuous monitoring and frequent vitals
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor and continuous SpO2
Two large-bore IVs; warmed fluids/blood as needed
Tourniquet for uncontrolled extremity hemorrhage
Preserve and label amputated part per protocol
NPO in anticipation of OR
imaging: X-ray of stump and amputated part (for fracture/replant planning)
CXR if associated major trauma* if indicated
CT angiography for proximal limb vascular mapping* if indicated
Transfer for definitive imaging if unavailable
meds: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV if significant hemorrhage and <3 h
Fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV or morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV titrated
Cefazolin 2 g IV for wound prophylaxis
Tdap/Td if not up to date
reassess: Reassess hemorrhage control and tourniquet time
Monitor hemodynamics and pain control
Re-document neurovascular status of stump
Confirm amputated part properly preserved and accompanying patient
disposition: Emergent transfer to replantation/hand-surgery center with the amputated part
Coordinate rapid transport to minimize ischemia time
Surgery consult/acceptance before transfer
discharge: Rarely discharged; only for minor distal tip amputations not requiring replant after specialist input
Wound care and strict infection/return precautions
Tetanus status documented
followup: Hand/plastic surgery follow-up arranged
Wound recheck in 48 h for non-operative cases
Occupational therapy/rehab referral

redFlags: Uncontrolled hemorrhage or hemodynamic instability
Proximal/major limb amputation
Prolonged warm ischemia time threatening replant viability
Crush/avulsion mechanism with extensive tissue loss
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization before transfer; certify benefit > risk
FSED is non-operative: emergent appropriate-transfer to replant center with documented acceptance
Ensure amputated part transported with patient; document ischemia times
Report work-related injuries appropriately; preserve evidence if assault-related

name: Animal / Human Bite
summary: Bite wound with infection, deep-structure, and rabies/tetanus risk; closure decisions vary by site.

triage: Identify species, provocation, animal availability for observation, and rabies vaccination status
Assess wound location, depth, and joint/tendon involvement
Note clenched-fist 'fight bite' over MCP (high-risk for septic joint)
Document tetanus immunization status
nursing: Copious irrigation with normal saline
Neurovascular and tendon function exam distal to wound
Pain control before exploration
Photograph/document wound* if indicated
keyOrders: High-pressure wound irrigation and debridement of devitalized tissue
Explore for tendon/joint/bone involvement (esp. hand)
Tetanus prophylaxis order
Rabies risk assessment and PEP initiation* if indicated
X-ray for fracture/tooth fragment/air in joint* if indicated
labs: CBC and inflammatory markers if systemic infection* if indicated
Blood cultures if febrile/septic* if indicated
imaging: X-ray for suspected fracture, retained tooth, or joint air (fight bite)
Ultrasound for abscess/foreign body* if indicated
CT/MRI for deep-space or osteomyelitis concern* if indicated
No imaging for superficial uncomplicated bites
meds: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID (first-line prophylaxis/treatment)
Cefuroxime + metronidazole or doxycycline + metronidazole if penicillin-allergic
Rabies PEP: RIG 20 IU/kg infiltrated + vaccine days 0,3,7,14 if indicated
Tdap/Td +/- TIG per immunization status
reassess: Reassess for adequate irrigation and infection signs
Recheck neurovascular/tendon status
Determine closure vs leaving open (delayed primary closure for high-risk)
Confirm rabies/tetanus decisions documented
disposition: Discharge most low-risk bites on prophylaxis
Refer/transfer hand surgery for fight bite, joint/tendon involvement, or deep infection
Admit for IV antibiotics if cellulitis/sepsis
discharge: Wound care and prophylactic antibiotics as prescribed
Generally do not primarily close cat bites/hand bites/high-risk wounds
Return precautions: spreading redness, pus, fever, increasing pain
followup: Wound recheck in 24-48 h (sooner for hand)
Complete rabies PEP series on schedule
Hand surgery follow-up for complex wounds

redFlags: Fight-bite over MCP with joint penetration (septic arthritis risk)
Spreading cellulitis, systemic toxicity, or sepsis
Deep hand/tendon/joint involvement
High-risk rabies exposure (bat, wild carnivore, unprovoked attack)
compliance: EMTALA MSE for all bite presentations
Mandatory reporting of animal bites to Texas/local health authority for rabies surveillance
Report suspected human-bite assault or abuse per Texas mandatory reporting
FSED appropriate-transfer if surgical/specialist care exceeds capability; document rabies decisions

name: Soft-Tissue Foreign Body
summary: Embedded object (glass/metal/wood/organic) requiring localization, removal decision, and infection prevention.

triage: Identify material (glass/metal vs organic/wood), entry site, and time since injury
Assess for neurovascular/tendon involvement and joint proximity
Determine if object is high-risk (organic, near vital structure)
Document tetanus immunization status
nursing: Clean and irrigate entry site
Distal neurovascular and motor check
Pain control before exploration
Prepare removal/imaging as indicated
keyOrders: Wound irrigation and gentle exploration
Neurovascular and tendon function exam
Imaging to localize foreign body
Tetanus prophylaxis order
Local anesthesia for removal* if indicated
labs: CBC/inflammatory markers if infected or retained long-standing* if indicated
Wound culture if signs of infection* if indicated
Coagulation panel if anticoagulated and deep removal planned* if indicated
imaging: Plain X-ray (detects glass >2 mm and most metal)
Ultrasound for radiolucent organic material (wood, thorns, plastic)
CT for deep or non-localized foreign body* if indicated
Avoid MRI if metallic FB suspected
meds: Lidocaine 1% local anesthesia for removal
Cephalexin 500 mg PO QID for contaminated/retained organic FB* if indicated
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO or ibuprofen 600 mg PO for pain
Tdap/Td if not up to date
reassess: Confirm complete removal (re-image if multiple/fragmenting)
Recheck neurovascular/tendon status after removal
Assess wound for closure vs leaving open
Decide on retained deep FB referral if removal unsuccessful
disposition: Discharge most after successful removal
Refer to surgery/hand/ortho for deep, periarticular, or hard-to-localize FB
Leave inert deep asymptomatic FB and refer if removal high-risk
discharge: Wound care and infection precautions
Explain that some inert FBs may be left and observed
Return precautions: redness, swelling, pus, increasing pain, fever
followup: Wound recheck in 48-72 h
Surgery/specialist follow-up for retained or deep FB
Imaging follow-up if FB intentionally left in place

redFlags: Foreign body near vessel, nerve, tendon, or joint
Signs of deep-space infection or abscess
Organic/wood FB (high infection/granuloma risk) that cannot be fully removed
Retained FB causing neurovascular compromise
compliance: EMTALA MSE for all presentations regardless of ability to pay
FSED appropriate-transfer if specialist removal beyond capability
Report assault- or abuse-related injuries per Texas mandatory reporting
Document tetanus status, removal attempts, and any retained FB disclosed to patient
